title: Essential Kids Bed Frame
summary: A short-legged twin bed frame with a taller three-rail headboard and a simple two-rail footboard — built top-down on both ends so the spacing lands right without a story stick.
description:
A kid's first "big bed" frame doesn't need much: two end assemblies, two siderails, and a cleat-and-slat system underneath. What makes this one easy to get right is the assembly order — build the footboard and headboard by working from the TOP rail down, using scrap 2x4 blocks as spacers between rails instead of measuring gaps by tape. That's faster and it's self-correcting: if a scrap spacer doesn't fit, you catch the problem before the frame is screwed together, not after.

The headboard carries three rails to the footboard's two, which is what gives it the taller, more finished look at the head of the bed without any extra joinery — it's the same butt-and-screw construction, just one more board.

Prefinish the headboard, footboard, and both siderails BEFORE final assembly. Once the frame is screwed together in the room, there's no good angle to get a brush or rag into the inside corners. The 2x2 cleats and slats don't need finishing — they're structural, not seen.

Space screws generously across each joint but keep them back about 3/4" from any edge; construction lumber this size splits easily if a screw lands too close to the end grain. Slightly angled screws bite harder than perfectly perpendicular ones and are worth the extra care per joint.

Cut list here is TWIN. Twin XL adds 5" to the siderails, cleats, and adds two extra slats — noted at each affected part. A full-size version needs wider rails (54" instead of 38") and a center support rail running the length of the frame with its own leg, since an unsupported full-size mattress will sag noticeably by the first week.

materials.name: 1x4, 6 ft
materials.note: Slats. Add one more board (and four more short screws) for a Twin XL frame.

cutList.note: Top and bottom rail. Full size: 54".
Top, middle, and bottom rail. Full size: 54".
Twin XL: 81-1/2", attached to the inside face of the siderails.
Twin XL: cut two additional slats. Full size adds a center support rail with its own leg — not included in this twin cut list.

steps.title: Build the footboard
steps.body: Work from the top down. On a flat, level surface, attach the two footboard legs to the top 2x6 rail with two 2-1/2" screws per end — back edges flush. Use scrap 2x4 blocks as spacers to hold the lower rail at the right height while you attach it with two more screws per end. The scrap block is faster and more consistent than measuring the gap by tape each time.

steps.title: Build the headboard
steps.body: Repeat the footboard sequence for the headboard, starting at the top rail and working down — but this end gets three rails instead of two, spaced with the same scrap-block method, which is what gives the headboard its taller, more finished profile.

steps.title: Prefinish, then join with the siderails
steps.body:
Prefinish the headboard, footboard, and both siderails now — once the frame is assembled in the bedroom there's no good angle to reach the inside corners with a brush.

In the room, attach the siderails to the headboard and footboard legs with the 2-1/2" screws, keeping both ends level as you go. Space screws wide across the joint but stay about 3/4" back from any edge — this lumber splits if a screw lands too close to the end grain. Driving screws at a slight angle adds real holding strength over dead-straight ones.

steps.title: Add the cleats and lay the slats
steps.body: Screw the 2x2 cleats to the inside face of both siderails with the longer screws, then lay the slats across the cleats and drive two of the shorter 1-5/8" screws through each slat into the cleat below.

images.alt: Simple twin bed frame with a taller three-rail headboard and low footboard
